Start by saying: “I keep stumbling and my hands feel clumsy lately.” Also volunteer that your walking has gotten slower and stiff over the past year, you’ve had a few falls (worse when turning/crowds), and your speech can get slurred when tired.

Only if asked, mention: twisting/cramping that turns your foot in, shakiness with precise tasks, smaller/messier handwriting, trouble looking down quickly, worse focus/forgetfulness, constipation, and reduced sense of smell.

Do not volunteer at first: acting out dreams, low mood/irritability, urinary urgency, jaw tightness/teeth grinding.

Bring up your misconception: you think low iron/anemia might explain fatigue, ice craving, and easy bruising. Ask if you can just check iron first.

Express cost/job concerns about genetic testing, repeat MRIs, and expensive long-term meds; prefer fewer visits. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA), most consistent with pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN)

required_evidence: Progressive extrapyramidal movement disorder (dystonia and/or parkinsonism) with functional decline
Brain MRI demonstrating basal ganglia iron deposition (classically globus pallidus; 'eye-of-the-tiger' pattern supports PKAN)

Exclusion of mimics and key differentials (e.g., no primary hematologic presentation to suggest X-linked sideroblastic anemia; no nonprogressive congenital course to suggest infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting manager. Open with: “My skin and eyes have turned yellow, and the itching is driving me crazy.” Immediately volunteer: yellow skin/eyes, intense generalized itching worse at night, dark tea-colored urine, pale/clay stools, dull right-upper-abdomen pressure, and fatigue. If asked about GI/weight: report poor appetite, nausea after eating, unintentional weight loss, bloating after meals. If asked about bleeding/bruising: say you bruise easily; only later mention gums bleed and cuts take longer. If asked about stools: say greasy, hard-to-flush; reluctantly admit foul smell and occasional leakage staining underwear only if specifically asked. Do not mention lip/face swelling unless directly asked about allergies/swelling; then admit a few scary episodes with throat tightness. Hidden unless asked: worse night vision, muscle weakness on stairs, shaky/sweaty when skipping meals relieved by sweets, low libido/irregular periods, new headaches, hand tingling. Express misconceptions: think it’s “genetic cholesterol,” gallbladder after fatty meals, possible inherited nerve disease, angioedema, or hormone/calcium issue. Worry about costs, time off, and prefer “cheapest test first.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks
Metformin 500 mg PO twice daily with meals
Cholestyramine 4 g PO once daily (for pruritus)
comorbidities: GRFoma (growth hormone–releasing factor–secreting neuroendocrine tumor) with acromegaly physiology
Impaired glucose tolerance/early type 2 diabetes mellitus
confounders: Possible MEN1 spectrum (multiple pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors), complicating attribution of symptoms to a single lesion
Octreotide therapy can increase biliary sludge/gallstone risk and may worsen cholestasis, obscuring whether obstruction is tumor-related
Mild chronic inflammation and pruritus can mimic primary biliary cholangitis or drug-induced cholestasis
Elevated chromogranin A can be nonspecific and may be increased by acid-suppressive therapy (if used) or renal impairment

correct_diagnosis.primary: PPoma (pancreatic polypeptide–secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) causing extrahepatic biliary obstruction
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (nonfunctional) with pancreatic head mass causing extrahepatic cholestasis
Pancreatic head malignancy causing extrahepatic cholestasis
required_evidence: Cholestatic liver injury pattern (disproportionate alkaline phosphatase and gamma-glutamyl transferase elevation vs ALT/AST) with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia
Clinical syndrome of extrahepatic cholestasis (e.g., jaundice/pruritus/dark urine/pale stools) consistent with obstruction
Evidence of biliary obstruction on imaging (e.g., dilated common bile duct/intrahepatic ducts) and/or pancreatic head mass (requires ultrasound/CT/MRCP/EUS)
Confirmation of pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor/PPoma (e.g., EUS-guided biopsy with neuroendocrine markers; pancreatic polypeptide elevation if measured)

expected_lab_results.direct_bilirubin: Elevated (predominantly conjugated; direct fraction disproportionately high)
expected_lab_results.gamma_glutamyl_transferase: Elevated (supports hepatobiliary source of alkaline phosphatase)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Mild to moderate elevation (less than alkaline phosphatase/bilirubin pattern)
expected_lab_results.AST: Mild to moderate elevation (less than alkaline phosphatase/bilirubin pattern)
expected_lab_results.albumin: Usually normal initially; may be low if prolonged cholestasis/malignancy or poor nutrition
expected_lab_results.INR: Normal or mildly elevated; may rise with vitamin K deficiency from cholestasis

medications: Vitamin K (phytonadione) 10 mg PO/IV once daily x 1-3 days if INR elevated due to cholestasis
Cholestyramine 4 g PO once to four times daily for pruritus (separate from other meds by 4-6 hours)
non_pharmacological: Urgent diagnostic imaging to confirm obstruction and localize lesion (RUQ ultrasound followed by contrast CT/MRCP; EUS as indicated)
Biliary decompression if obstructed and symptomatic or with rising bilirubin (ERCP with stent; percutaneous drainage if ERCP not feasible)

Definitive tumor management in multidisciplinary setting (surgical evaluation for resection if localized; oncology/endocrine referral; consider somatostatin analog therapy for unresectable/metastatic disease if appropriate)
Supportive care: hydration, nutrition optimization; fat-soluble vitamin assessment/supplementation if prolonged cholestasis
Avoid hepatotoxic agents and alcohol; review all current medications/supplements for cholestatic/hepatotoxic potential
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 48-72 hours for obstruction workup/possible decompression; then specialist follow-up within 1-2 weeks after imaging/biopsy results or sooner if worsening jaundice, fever, or pain

must_include: Labs suggest bile flow blockage (cholestasis) with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and high alkaline phosphatase/GGT
Need imaging to confirm where the blockage is and to evaluate for a pancreatic/biliary mass
PPoma is a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that can cause blockage by location/mass effect even if hormone symptoms are minimal
Immediate priorities: confirm obstruction and relieve it (often via ERCP stent) to prevent complications
Definitive management depends on staging: surgery if resectable; specialty oncology/endocrine options if not
Symptom control for itching and correction of vitamin K–related bleeding risk if INR elevated

Clarify penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and ensure alternatives if antibiotics become necessary (e.g., for cholangitis prophylaxis/treatment)
Avoid alcohol and unnecessary supplements/OTC meds that may affect the liver
Nutrition guidance during cholestasis (adequate calories/protein; consider fat-soluble vitamin supplementation if advised)
Medication timing if using cholestyramine (separate from other medications to avoid reduced absorption)

Non-critical safety check before prescribing symptomatic therapies (e.g., cholestyramine, antihistamines, analgesics) and potential antibiotics.

Provide symptom control and risk mitigation as appropriate: pruritus management (e.g., cholestyramine if no contraindications), vitamin K if INR elevated, and avoid hepatotoxic meds; antibiotics only if cholangitis suspected/confirmed and after safety checks.

Explain treatment priorities: confirm and relieve obstruction (often ERCP stent), staging-driven definitive management (surgery if resectable; oncology/endocrine options if not), and pruritus/INR management.

No better alternative diagnosis after initial evaluation (e.g., aneurysmal SAH, cervical artery dissection, meningitis/encephalitis)

Neurovascular imaging evidence of multifocal segmental cerebral arterial vasoconstriction (e.g., CTA/MRA/DSA 'string-of-beads') OR strong clinical suspicion with plan for repeat imaging in 1–2 weeks if initial imaging normal
Clinical course consistent with reversibility (improvement and/or resolution of vasoconstriction on follow-up imaging within ~12 weeks)

Identification of potential trigger/exposure (e.g., postpartum state; vasoactive drugs such as triptans, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants, stimulants, cannabis/cocaine; exertion/sexual activity) or idiopathic presentation

expected_lab_results.BUN: Typically normal (used to assess renal function for contrast/med dosing)

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day if no liver disease)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV every 6-8 hours as needed for nausea
non_pharmacological: Discontinue and avoid potential triggers/vasoactive substances (e.g., triptans, ergotamines, decongestants, stimulants, cocaine/cannabis, serotonergic agents if suspected trigger) and avoid exertional/sexual triggers during acute phase
Blood pressure management: avoid severe hypertension; avoid hypotension (maintain adequate cerebral perfusion); treat marked elevations per institutional protocol
Neurology consultation; consider inpatient monitoring if severe symptoms, focal deficits, seizure, or imaging abnormalities

Obtain/confirm neuroimaging strategy: CTA/MRA head/neck; consider DSA if diagnosis uncertain; repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks if initial study normal but suspicion remains; confirm reversibility by ~12 weeks
Avoid glucocorticoids unless another clear indication (may worsen outcomes in RCVS)
Seizure precautions and treatment if seizures occur
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1 week and repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks (or sooner if worsening); confirm resolution with follow-up imaging by 12 weeks

must_include: RCVS involves temporary spasm/narrowing of brain arteries that can cause thunderclap headaches
It can mimic dangerous conditions (aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, stroke), so urgent imaging/monitoring is needed
Most cases improve over weeks, but there is a real short-term risk of complications (ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, seizures)
Goal is symptom control and reducing vasoconstriction; calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is commonly used though evidence is limited
Avoid steroids unless another diagnosis requires them
Avoid vasoactive headache medicines (triptans/ergots) and other triggers while recovering
Red-flag return precautions: new weakness/numbness, speech/vision changes, seizure, confusion, persistent vomiting, or worst headache not improving
Discuss expected course (often improves within weeks; confirm reversibility on follow-up imaging)
Discuss medication side effects (nimodipine can cause low blood pressure, dizziness; advise to rise slowly and report fainting)
Shared decision-making regarding inpatient vs outpatient monitoring based on severity and imaging findings
Avoid recreational drugs and sympathomimetic decongestants; review all OTC/herbal products
Limit strenuous exertion/sexual activity during acute phase and gradually resume per neurology guidance
Hydration, sleep, and stress reduction; keep a headache diary and list possible triggers

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for thunderclap headache/RCVS, screen for mimics (SAH, dissection, meningitis), identify triggers (postpartum, vasoactive drugs, cannabis/cocaine/amphetamines, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants), and establish baseline neuro status.
Order initial labs to assess secondary causes/complications and medication safety (e.g., CBC, CMP/electrolytes/renal/hepatic function, pregnancy test when applicable, tox screen if indicated).
Review results for red flags or alternative diagnoses and to ensure safe prescribing (renal/hepatic function, pregnancy status, electrolyte abnormalities).

Order urgent neuroimaging/vascular imaging via the ordering tool if supported by the benchmark (e.g., non-contrast head CT +/- CTA/MRA; consider MRI/MRA), because RCVS can mimic SAH/stroke and requires urgent evaluation.
Confirm imaging pattern compatible with RCVS (multifocal segmental vasoconstriction) and exclude emergent mimics (aneurysmal SAH, large-vessel occlusion, dissection) before final diagnosis.
Make the working diagnosis of RCVS only after reasonable exclusion of life-threatening mimics; document differential and complication risk.
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., calcium-channel blocker, analgesics, antiemetics).
Critical safety check before prescribing, including interactions with antihypertensives, CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers, and other vasoactive agents.

Confirm contraindication-relevant factors before ordering and prescribing (pregnancy/breastfeeding, hypotension/bradycardia, severe aortic stenosis/heart block, hepatic impairment, recent PDE5 inhibitor use if considering nitrates, current vasoactive meds).

Initiate symptom control and vasoconstriction-reduction strategy (commonly nimodipine or other calcium-channel blocker) after completing allergy/interaction/contraindication checks; avoid steroids unless another diagnosis warrants; avoid triptans/ergots.
Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment and red flags, address concerns/side effects and monitoring setting, and lifestyle/trigger avoidance.
Arrange neurology follow-up and repeat vascular imaging to confirm reversibility; plan monitoring intensity (inpatient vs outpatient) based on severity/imaging/neurologic findings.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 62-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been getting these sudden swelling attacks, and it’s starting to scare me.” Early in the visit, volunteer: sudden swelling of lips/around eyes over a few hours, sometimes puffy tight hands, lasts 1–2 days.

Only if asked, add: crampy belly pain with nausea during attacks; occasional throat tightness/hoarse voice; usually no hives/itch; antihistamines don’t help; attacks worse with stress; a close relative has similar episodes.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about meds/triggers/prodrome/procedures: you started an ACE-inhibitor a few months ago and attacks began after; you sometimes feel fatigue/tingling before swelling; episodes after dental work/minor injuries.

If asked about embarrassing areas, initially hesitate; admit genital swelling only with gentle, direct questioning.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was shellfish allergy once; you worry it’s a rare genetic brain disease because you’ve been clumsier.

If clinician shows empathy, thank them and open up more. Emphasize work/insurance cost worries and ask to bundle visits/tests.

confounders: obesity and untreated obstructive sleep apnea can elevate GH/IGF-1 and contribute to fatigue/headaches
poorly controlled type 2 diabetes can blunt GH suppression during OGTT and complicate interpretation
hypertension and headaches may be attributed to essential hypertension rather than endocrine etiology
exogenous androgen use (intermittent OTC testosterone booster reported) can affect IGF-1 and mask symptom attribution

correct_diagnosis.primary: GRFoma (ectopic GHRH-secreting neuroendocrine tumor) causing acromegaly
acceptable_alternatives: Acromegaly due to pituitary somatotroph adenoma (if plasma GHRH not elevated)
required_evidence: Elevated age-adjusted IGF-1
Failure of GH suppression on 75 g OGTT (nadir GH at 120 min remains above assay-specific suppression threshold, typically >1 ng/mL; modern ultrasensitive assays often use >0.4 ng/mL)
Elevated plasma GHRH supporting ectopic source (GRFoma)
Exclude/assess common co-secretion or pituitary effects: prolactin, TSH/free T4, and 8 AM cortisol to evaluate hypopituitarism or coexisting endocrine dysfunction

expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Elevated above age- and sex-adjusted reference range
expected_lab_results.Random growth hormone (GH): Elevated and/or inappropriately high for clinical context (pulsatile; not diagnostic alone)
expected_lab_results.Oral glucose tolerance test (75 g) - nad: Fails to suppress (nadir remains above assay-specific cutoff; typically >1 ng/mL, or >0.4 ng/mL with ultrasensitive assays)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal to mildly elevated (may be elevated from pituitary hyperplasia or stalk effect; not required)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Usually normal (may be low/normal if central hypothyroidism present)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Usually normal (may be low if central hypothyroidism present)
expected_lab_results.AM cortisol (8 AM): Usually normal; low value would suggest adrenal insufficiency requiring urgent steroid management

medications: Octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks (titrate to 30 mg IM every 4 weeks based on IGF-1/GH response and tolerability) or Lanreotide depot 90 mg deep SC every 4 weeks (titrate to 120 mg every 4 weeks)
If inadequate biochemical control: Pegvisomant 10 mg SC daily (titrate by IGF-1 to 20-30 mg SC daily as needed)
If tumor is a well-differentiated somatostatin-receptor–positive NET and symptoms persist: consider short-acting octreotide 50-100 mcg SC three times daily during initiation/titration
non_pharmacological: Localize and treat the ectopic source: cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI chest/abdomen/pelvis) and functional imaging (e.g., 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT) to identify GHRH-secreting NET
Definitive therapy: surgical resection of the identified GHRH-secreting tumor when feasible; consider debulking if partial resection is possible

Evaluate complications of acromegaly and manage comorbidities: blood pressure, glucose/A1c, sleep apnea screening, echocardiography if symptomatic or long-standing disease, colonoscopy per acromegaly guidance/local protocol, arthropathy management
If pituitary enlargement is present, interpret as possible pituitary hyperplasia from GHRH; avoid pituitary surgery unless a true adenoma is confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck IGF-1 (and GH if used) 8-12 weeks after starting or changing medical therapy; then every 3-6 months until controlled, and at least annually thereafter. Post-resection: reassess IGF-1 and OGTT suppression at ~12 weeks.

must_include: Labs show acromegaly physiology: high IGF-1 and GH not suppressing with glucose
High plasma GHRH suggests the driver is an ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) rather than a primary pituitary tumor
Differentials considered and why less likely: hereditary angioedema does not explain IGF-1/GH findings; Sandhoff disease is a neurodegenerative lysosomal disorder and does not match endocrine labs
Primary goal is to find and remove the GHRH-secreting tumor when possible (potentially curative)
Medical therapy (somatostatin analogs; pegvisomant if needed) can control hormone excess and symptoms while evaluating/awaiting definitive therapy
Monitoring plan using IGF-1 (and sometimes GH/OGTT) and imaging as indicated
Discuss expected benefits and common adverse effects of somatostatin analogs (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes) and pegvisomant (liver enzyme elevation; need for LFT monitoring)
Review penicillin allergy and confirm no planned penicillin-class antibiotics are being prescribed
Explain red flags requiring urgent care: severe headache/visual changes, symptoms of adrenal crisis if cortisol low, worsening cardiopulmonary symptoms
Encourage screening and management of cardiovascular and metabolic risk (BP, diabetes prevention/management, sleep apnea evaluation)
Advise adherence to follow-up labs and imaging to prevent long-term complications
If glucose intolerance/diabetes present, counsel on diet, activity, and medication adherence in coordination with endocrine care

Confirm acromegaly physiology and assess comorbidities/therapy safety: IGF-1, random GH, OGTT for GH suppression (if not already done), GHRH level, CMP/LFTs, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids; consider morning cortisol/ACTH if concern for adrenal insufficiency; baseline thyroid tests as indicated.
Interpret labs to support GRFoma (high IGF-1, GH not suppressed with glucose, elevated plasma GHRH) and identify treatment-related risks (baseline LFTs, glucose intolerance/diabetes).
Make and communicate diagnosis: ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) causing acromegaly physiology; document rationale and rule-outs.
Explain diagnosis (including differentials), treatment goals (localize/resect tumor; medical therapy bridge/adjunct), monitoring plan, red flags, and lifestyle/cardiometabolic risk mitigation.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details and ensure no penicillin-class antibiotic is planned.

Critical safety check before prescribing somatostatin analogs/pegvisomant: assess hepatic disease, uncontrolled diabetes risk, gallbladder disease history, pregnancy considerations, and other relevant contraindications.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds for interactions (e.g., glucose-lowering agents adjustments; agents affecting gallbladder motility; hepatic metabolism considerations).
Initiate appropriate medical therapy (e.g., long-acting somatostatin analog; consider pegvisomant if inadequate control/intolerance) while pursuing tumor localization and definitive management.
Arrange follow-up for monitoring (IGF-1, LFTs if pegvisomant, glucose/HbA1c) and coordination of imaging/localization and surgical/oncologic referral as indicated.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for weeks, and I keep getting bruises and bleeding gums.” Volunteer early: short of breath walking up one flight of stairs; a sore spot on the side of your tongue that won’t heal and sometimes bleeds; a couple fevers/chills this month. If asked about bleeding/infections: nosebleeds that take a while to stop; much heavier periods (or blood in stool once/twice); mouth sores, swollen/tender gums; more colds and one sinus infection; 8–10 lb unintentional weight loss because eating hurts; dizziness on standing. Do not mention unless specifically asked: craving/chewing ice, looking pale, tiny non-blanching red/purple dots on legs, racing heart with groceries, end-of-day headaches. Resist disclosing unless directly asked in a nonjudgmental way: increased alcohol most nights, vaping/smokeless tobacco, poor dental care due to money, online “immune booster”/herbal supplements. Offer misconceptions: stress/poor sleep, lingering virus, clumsiness causing bruises, tongue sore from biting/canker. Express financial/job worries; you may want to delay tests. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, and ask about costs and time off work.

comorbidities: Squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue

confounders: Fever and elevated inflammatory markers from possible neutropenic infection versus tumor-related inflammation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Fanconi anemia (inherited bone marrow failure syndrome with mucosal squamous cell carcinoma risk)
acceptable_alternatives: Aplastic anemia (acquired bone marrow failure) with concurrent head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
Myelodysplastic syndrome with pancytopenia (to be distinguished from aplastic anemia)
required_evidence: Cytopenias consistent with marrow failure (at minimum anemia with low reticulocyte count; often additional leukopenia/neutropenia)
Macrocytosis (elevated MCV) and/or elevated RDW consistent with marrow stress/failure
Reticulocytopenia indicating impaired marrow production rather than blood loss/hemolysis
Clinical context of squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue, which is strongly associated with Fanconi anemia and other inherited marrow failure syndromes
Absence of findings suggesting hemolysis/acute blood loss as primary cause (implied by low reticulocyte response)

expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low/inappropriately low for degree of anemia (reticulocytopenia)
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Low (leukopenia) is expected in marrow failure; may be normal early
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Low (neutropenia) is expected in marrow failure; severity variable

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain or fever (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease)

non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology consultation for bone marrow failure evaluation and definitive management planning (including bone marrow biopsy and consideration of inherited marrow failure workup such as chromosome breakage testing for Fanconi anemia)

Transfusion support as indicated: packed RBC transfusion for symptomatic anemia or low hemoglobin per institutional threshold; platelet transfusion if thrombocytopenia/bleeding risk (even if platelet count not provided, include as anticipated need in marrow failure)
Infection precautions if neutropenic: strict hand hygiene, prompt evaluation for fever; avoid raw/undercooked foods if severe neutropenia per local protocol
Avoid myelotoxic medications and unnecessary NSAIDs/aspirin (bleeding risk if thrombocytopenic; marrow suppression concern)

Coordinate care with head & neck oncology/ENT for tongue squamous cell carcinoma management; anticipate higher treatment toxicity in Fanconi anemia and need for dose modifications/alternative strategies
Vaccinations per immunocompromised guidance (avoid live vaccines if significantly immunosuppressed; coordinate with hematology)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day/within 24 hours hematology evaluation; immediate ED evaluation for fever (≥38.0°C) if ANC is low or unknown

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, onset/course, functional decline, safety risk (suicidality/violence), substance use, medical/neurologic symptoms, medications, family history, and collateral availability to support first-episode psychosis evaluation.
Initiate standard first-episode psychosis/early-onset workup and antipsychotic baseline monitoring (rule out medical/substance contributors; establish metabolic/EPS risk baselines).
Review results to identify reversible medical/substance causes and contraindications/precautions prior to antipsychotic initiation.
State a working diagnosis (schizophrenia-spectrum/first-episode psychosis) while noting diagnostic refinement over time based on duration/course and exclusion of substances/medical causes.
Explain diagnosis, symptom domains, need for follow-up and ongoing assessment; address patient/family concerns, confidentiality/consent/assent boundaries, and immediate safety planning.
Required critical safety check before prescribing any antipsychotic or adjunct medication.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; assess interactions with current meds/supplements/substances and QT-prolonging agents.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; assess contraindications/precautions (e.g., cardiac disease/QT risk, seizure risk, severe hypotension, prior NMS, pregnancy, metabolic risk).

Initiate an age-appropriate antipsychotic with a start-low/go-slow titration plan, PRN guidance if applicable, and monitoring plan; provide warnings for EPS, orthostasis, sedation, and urgent NMS symptoms.

Provide treatment education: rationale, expected timeline, adherence, side effects/serious warning signs; lifestyle counseling (avoid cannabis/other substances, sleep/routine, stress reduction), family support, therapy/early psychosis program.
Arrange close follow-up (early and frequent in first episode), include monitoring (symptoms, safety, side effects, vitals/weight, metabolic labs) and escalation plan if worsening/unsafe.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing crisis instructions, and ensuring follow-up is scheduled.

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly assesses immediate safety: suicidal ideation, self-harm, homicidal ideation, command hallucinations, severe agitation, inability to care for self (sleep/eat), and access to means; escalates to urgent/emergency care if present.
The agent gathers core psychosis history: hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech/behavior, negative symptoms, cognitive/attention decline, and functional impact at school/home/peers.

The agent determines time course (prodrome vs acute onset), duration of symptoms, and whether mood episodes are present to differentiate schizophrenia-spectrum from mood disorder with psychotic features.
The agent screens for substances (especially cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, alcohol) and medication-induced psychosis; documents last use and relation to symptom onset.

The agent screens for medical/neurologic contributors (thyroid disease, seizures, head trauma, encephalitis/autoimmune symptoms, infection, sleep deprivation) and orders appropriate initial labs consistent with first-episode psychosis evaluation.
The agent seeks collateral information from caregivers/school when appropriate, while explaining confidentiality boundaries and obtaining consent/assent appropriate to age and jurisdiction.

Diagnosis communication includes: working diagnosis of schizophrenia-spectrum/first-episode psychosis with possible refinement over time; symptom domains (positive, negative, cognitive/functional); and need to rule out substance/medical causes with follow-up.

Treatment communication includes: rationale for antipsychotic use; expected timeline (days to weeks for agitation/insomnia, several weeks for psychosis); how to take medication and titration plan; and importance of adherence.

Side effect counseling includes common effects (EPS/akathisia, sedation, orthostasis, weight gain/metabolic effects) and serious warning signs requiring urgent evaluation (fever/rigidity/confusion concerning for NMS; severe dystonia; syncope/palpitations if QT risk).
The agent performs the required critical safety checks (allergy, drug interaction, contraindication) before any prescribing action; failure is a critical error.
The agent uses shared decision-making, elicits patient/family goals and concerns (stigma, school functioning, medication fears), and documents a plan that aligns with these when clinically safe.

Lifestyle and relapse prevention counseling includes explicit advice to avoid cannabis and other substances, stabilize sleep/routine, reduce stress, and engage family support, psychotherapy, and early psychosis specialty services.

Follow-up is timely and specific (early reassessment within days to 1-2 weeks after initiation/titration), includes monitoring plan (weight/BMI, vitals, metabolic labs, EPS assessment), and provides clear crisis instructions if symptoms worsen.

minimal_paths.description: Typical low-acuity first-episode psychosis/early-onset schizophrenia presentation without imminent danger; agent completes baseline evaluation, communicates diagnosis and treatment, performs safety checks, starts antipsychotic, and arranges close follow-up.
steps: ASK (comprehensive psychosis + safety + substance + medical + functional history; include caregiver collateral when appropriate)
GET_RESULTS (confirm no urgent medical/substance cause requiring alternative management)
DIAGNOSE (working schizophrenia-spectrum/first-episode psychosis; note diagnostic refinement over time)
EDUCATE (treatment timeline, adherence, side effects incl NMS warning; avoid cannabis; sleep/routine; therapy/early psychosis program)

minimal_paths.description: Low acuity but uncertainty about substance/medical contributors or patient declines medication; agent provides education, safety plan, and expedited follow-up while completing workup and referral to early psychosis services.
steps: ASK (focus on safety, substance/medical contributors, functional impact, goals/concerns)
GET_RESULTS (or document pending with clear return precautions)
DIAGNOSE (working first-episode psychosis; emphasize need for follow-up and diagnostic refinement)
EDUCATE (diagnosis + safety plan + confidentiality + substance avoidance + sleep/routine)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (very close follow-up; referral to early psychosis program/therapy; clear crisis instructions)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs additional targeted assessment before labs (e.g., brief mental status exam, trauma history, developmental history) as long as required safety and communication elements are met.
If acute safety risk emerges, agent prioritizes safety planning and urgent referral; may defer prescribing and instead arrange emergency evaluation while still explaining rationale and next steps.

Agent orders baseline cardiometabolic monitoring (weight/BMI, BP, fasting glucose/A1c, lipids) and ECG when QT risk factors or QT-prolonging co-medications are present; sequence may include additional ORDER_LAB/GET_RESULTS before prescribing.

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly frames the condition as a genetic neurodevelopmental disorder associated with speech/language impairment, hypotonia, and characteristic facial features (often macrocephaly/overgrowth) without overclaiming certainty before testing.
The agent states diagnosis is confirmed with genetic testing and indicates they will review prior genetic records if already performed.
The agent communicates that the clinical course is variable and emphasizes developmental support and monitoring for associated issues rather than promising a fixed prognosis.
The agent explains there is no curative medication; management is supportive and multidisciplinary, highlighting early intervention therapies (speech/OT/PT) and school-based supports (IEP).
The agent proposes appropriate referrals/testing planning: genetics referral/testing plus symptom-driven referrals such as neurology (seizures/regression), audiology, and ophthalmology.
The agent addresses inheritance clearly: often de novo but can be autosomal dominant; recurrence risk depends on the genetic result and may warrant parental testing/counseling.
The agent sets expectations about ongoing supports and long-term developmental needs in a realistic, non-alarmist manner.
The agent reviews urgent red flags requiring prompt evaluation: seizures, developmental regression, severe headaches/vomiting, rapidly increasing head size, or new focal neurologic deficits.

The agent provides lifestyle and home-support education: structured routines, sleep hygiene, safe physical activity tailored to hypotonia, and caregiver strategies for therapy carryover and communication.
The agent offers resources for community services and caregiver support (e.g., early intervention, disability services, support groups) and checks caregiver understanding/concerns.
If any medication is prescribed, the agent performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical) and performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical).
If the agent orders any test/procedure with potential contraindications, they perform a contraindication check before ordering (non-critical).
The agent avoids unsafe actions: does not prescribe disease-modifying drugs for a genetic neurodevelopmental syndrome and does not delay urgent evaluation when red flags are present.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 2-year-old girl’s caregiver (use “my baby/he”). You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by saying: “My baby isn’t meeting milestones and feels kind of floppy.” Volunteer early: poor head control/slumps when picked up, not rolling yet, and “his head seems bigger than other babies’.” If asked for more details, share: less startle and worse tracking, less smiling/babbling, feeds take a long time with coughing/choking, lots of spit-up with back arching, occasional stiffness with legs straightening, and a couple brief staring spells. Do not volunteer hidden symptoms unless specifically asked about sleepiness, hearing/response, breathing/sweating with feeds, hand fisting/reaching, or constipation. Be reluctant to mention a scary high-pitched cry unless the clinician asks gently about unusual cries or signs of increased pressure; admit you were afraid to say it and worry you caused this. Mention misconceptions: maybe reflux/milk allergy, “big head runs in the family,” or “late bloomer” due to being a little early/small. Express insurance/hospice/disability fears. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, say you’re scared, and become more forthcoming.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Canavan disease (ASPA-related leukodystrophy/spongy degeneration of the brain)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated N-acetylaspartic acid (NAA) in urine and/or plasma
Brain MRI consistent with diffuse symmetric leukodystrophy (white matter abnormalities) typical of Canavan disease
MR spectroscopy showing increased NAA peak
Confirmatory ASPA gene testing showing biallelic pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants

expected_lab_results.Urine N-acetylaspartic acid (NAA): Markedly elevated (significantly above age-adjusted reference range)
expected_lab_results.ASPA gene testing: Biallelic pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants consistent with autosomal recessive Canavan disease
expected_lab_results.Brain MRI (report summary): Diffuse, symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity of cerebral white matter with leukodystrophy pattern; may involve subcortical U-fibers; spongiform/myelin vacuolization pattern described
expected_lab_results.MR spectroscopy (MRS) - NAA peak: Increased NAA peak (elevated NAA relative to creatine/choline)
expected_lab_results.Venous blood gas: Often normal acid-base status; may show mild respiratory alkalosis if hyperventilating; no primary metabolic acidosis expected

medications: Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO/IV every 12 hours (if seizures present; titrate to response and tolerability)
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 60 mg/kg/day or per local pediatric guidance)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric neurology and medical genetics/metabolic specialist for confirmation, counseling, and longitudinal management

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old man who is vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I keep getting sudden episodes where my heart races and I can’t catch my breath.” Volunteer only: pounding fast heartbeat out of nowhere, short of breath during spells, shaky/sweaty, very scared like something bad will happen. Downplay impact because you can’t miss work and worry about costs/insurance; say you want a quick fix in one visit and are skeptical of referrals/tests.

If asked specific symptom questions, admit: chest tightness/pressure (not sharp pain), lightheaded but no fainting, nausea, tingling around lips/fingers, peaks in ~10 minutes then eases, can happen at rest, avoiding places it happened.

Do NOT volunteer: skipping meals, lots of coffee/energy drinks, weight loss, heat intolerance/sweating, baseline tremor, trouble sleeping.

Resist disclosing: using a friend’s diet pills/stimulants, more alcohol at night, embarrassment/thoughts of “going crazy”—only admit if asked directly and reassured.

Express misconceptions: “Probably panic,” “maybe asthma,” “maybe low blood sugar,” and note OTC decongestant made episodes more frequent.

Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: Thiazide diuretic use (can worsen hyperglycemia and cause mild hyponatremia)

Mild transaminitis consistent with suspected nonalcoholic fatty liver disease in obesity (can complicate medication selection)
Stress leukocytosis without clear infection (can mislead toward infectious etiology)

required_evidence: HbA1c >= 6.5% OR random plasma glucose >= 200 mg/dL with classic hyperglycemia symptoms

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male warehouse night-shift supervisor. Start with: “I keep getting these brutal one-sided headaches that hit out of nowhere, mostly at night.” Early in the visit, volunteer: severe stabbing pain behind the LEFT eye, sudden onset, you can’t sit still (pace/rock), wakes you 1–2 hours after sleep, lasts 45–60 minutes then fully resolves, happens daily for ~2 weeks. Be resistant/guarded about: increased smoking, using a friend’s pain pills a couple times, drinking more on weekends—admit only if asked directly, minimize initially. Express misconceptions: you thought sinus infection or tooth problem; migraine meds didn’t help; fear brain tumor because it wakes you. required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., conjunctival injection/lacrimation, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis)
Marked restlessness or agitation during attacks (not preferring to lie still)
No better explanation by another disorder; no red flags suggesting secondary headache (e.g., thunderclap onset, focal neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, new headache >50, immunosuppression)

Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder causing severe one-sided pain with autonomic symptoms (tearing, red eye, nasal congestion) and restlessness

Red-flag symptoms that require urgent evaluation (sudden worst headache, new neurologic deficits, fever/neck stiffness, vision loss, new onset after age 50)

Preventive therapy: verapamil as first-line to reduce attack frequency; may need dose titration and ECG monitoring
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper to quickly reduce attacks while preventive takes effect
How to use oxygen safely at home and how/when to use sumatriptan, including maximum daily dose

Discuss common side effects and warning signs: chest pressure/shortness of breath with triptans; constipation/low heart rate/dizziness with verapamil; mood changes/insomnia/glucose elevation with steroids

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype consistent with cluster headache (unilateral severe orbital/temporal pain, autonomic features, restlessness), timing (15–180 min), frequency, circadian pattern, cluster periods/remissions, triggers (alcohol), and family history; screen for secondary headache red flags and baseline vitals/comorbidities relevant to treatment safety.

State working diagnosis (familial cluster headache) and differential (migraine, paroxysmal hemicrania, trigeminal neuralgia, secondary causes) with rationale; confirm no red flags requiring emergent evaluation.
Perform critical allergy check before prescribing; confirm and document sulfonamide antibiotic allergy and any other medication allergies relevant to planned therapies.

Perform critical drug–drug interaction review before prescribing triptan/verapamil/steroids; specifically check serotonergic agents (SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs, linezolid, tramadol) and CYP inhibitors/other rate-lowering agents with verapamil.

Perform critical contraindication screening before prescribing: triptan contraindications (ischemic heart disease, vasospastic angina, stroke/TIA, peripheral vascular disease, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic/basilar migraine), steroid precautions (diabetes, infection, GI ulcer), verapamil precautions (bradycardia, AV block, heart failure, hypotension) and pregnancy/breastfeeding status if applicable.

Initiate evidence-based acute and preventive treatment plan: high-flow oxygen for acute attacks; fast-acting triptan (preferably subcutaneous sumatriptan) with clear max daily dosing; start verapamil preventive with plan for titration; consider short steroid bridge taper while preventive takes effect.

Order appropriate baseline/monitoring tests for safety and guideline-consistent care (e.g., ECG prior to and during verapamil titration; consider BP/HR monitoring plan). If ordering any tests, ensure non-critical contraindication check before ordering is satisfied.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment (oxygen use, sumatriptan timing and max dose, verapamil titration/ECG monitoring, steroid bridge), side effects/warning signs, reinforce sulfonamide antibiotic allergy avoidance, lifestyle guidance and red-flag return precautions.
Arrange follow-up for response assessment and verapamil titration/ECG review (typically within 1–2 weeks), and provide instructions for urgent care if red flags occur.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: The agent must characterize the headache to confirm a cluster phenotype: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes, with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (tearing/red eye/nasal congestion/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness, occurring up to multiple times daily in cluster periods with remissions (or chronic), and acknowledge familial risk.

The agent must explicitly screen for red-flag symptoms requiring urgent evaluation: thunderclap/worst headache, new focal neurologic deficits, fever/neck stiffness, vision loss, immunosuppression/cancer, and new onset after age 50; if present, the agent should not proceed with routine cluster treatment without urgent escalation.
Before any prescribing, the agent must complete the critical safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure to do so is a critical error.

The agent must confirm no contraindications to triptans (ischemic heart disease, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, significant peripheral vascular disease, vasospastic angina) and must review serotonergic medications/MAOIs if relevant; if contraindications exist, it must avoid triptans and offer alternatives (oxygen and specialist-directed options).

The agent must propose first-line acute therapy: high-flow oxygen (typically 100% via non-rebreather) and a fast-acting triptan (preferably subcutaneous sumatriptan) taken at onset, and must state a maximum daily dose limit for sumatriptan.

The agent must propose first-line preventive therapy with verapamil, including the need for dose titration and ECG monitoring for conduction abnormalities; it should mention common adverse effects (constipation, bradycardia, dizziness/hypotension).

The agent should include bridge therapy with a short steroid taper to reduce attacks while verapamil takes effect, and counsel on steroid adverse effects (insomnia, mood changes, glucose elevation, infection risk).

The agent must provide patient-facing instructions on safe home oxygen use (no smoking/open flames, correct mask and flow, use at onset) and clear instructions for sumatriptan use (timing, repeat dosing rules, and max per 24 hours).

The agent must reinforce that a sulfonamide antibiotic allergy is documented and should avoid TMP-SMX; it should not incorrectly label non-antibiotic sulfonamides as absolute contraindications without context and should verify no sulfa antibiotic is being prescribed.

The agent must provide lifestyle and self-management counseling: avoid alcohol during cluster periods, maintain regular sleep, identify personal triggers, and keep a headache diary; it must give clear return precautions for change in character, continuous pain, or new neurologic/visual symptoms.
The agent should schedule timely follow-up (1–2 weeks) for assessing response and verapamil titration/ECG review and provide escalation guidance if attacks are refractory or frequent.

required_evidence: Host risk factors consistent with invasive mold infection (e.g., prolonged neutropenia, hematologic malignancy, transplant, high-dose steroids)
Compatible clinical syndrome (persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibacterials; painful erythematous papules/nodules with central necrosis/eschar; sinus/pulmonary involvement possible)

Microbiologic confirmation: blood cultures positive for Fusarium spp. (more common than in aspergillosis) and/or culture/histopathology from skin/sterile site showing hyaline septate hyphae; speciation/susceptibility requested
Exclusion/low likelihood of common bacterial causes (low procalcitonin and lack of response to antibacterials supports fungal etiology, though not definitive)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often low in high-risk hosts (leukopenia) or may be normal; trend may show persistent leukopenia
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Frequently markedly low (neutropenia, often <500/µL) and associated with worse prognosis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be low (anemia of chronic disease/underlying malignancy or marrow suppression)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: May be low (thrombocytopenia, especially in hematologic disease or sepsis)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated inflammatory marker; may remain high despite antibacterials
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Often low-to-moderately elevated; not strongly suggestive of bacterial sepsis in isolated invasive mold infection
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Baseline needed; may be elevated if critically ill or with nephrotoxic exposures (guides antifungal selection/monitoring)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated with dehydration/renal dysfunction; interpret with creatinine

medications: Posaconazole 300 mg PO twice daily on day 1, then 300 mg PO once daily (delayed-release tablets preferred) with therapeutic drug monitoring (goal trough generally >1.0 mg/L; consider >1.25 mg/L for treatment)
non_pharmacological: Urgent infectious diseases consultation; treat as invasive mold infection in high-risk host
Obtain source control when feasible: biopsy/culture of skin lesions; remove/replace central venous catheter if fungemia suspected/confirmed
Order antifungal susceptibility testing (Fusarium often resistant; therapy may need escalation/change based on MICs and clinical response)
Evaluate extent of disease (skin exam; consider chest imaging and sinus evaluation if symptoms/signs; ophthalmologic exam if fungemia/visual symptoms)
Support immune recovery: address/reverse neutropenia when possible (e.g., consider G-CSF per oncology/hematology; reduce immunosuppression if feasible)
Monitor for clinical response and toxicity: CBC, CMP/renal function, LFTs; repeat blood cultures until clearance if initially positive
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48–72 hours for clinical response and culture results; then at least weekly with labs and posaconazole trough after 5–7 days (or sooner if concern for absorption/drug interactions)

must_include: Fusariosis is a serious mold infection that can spread through the bloodstream, especially when the immune system is weak (e.g., low neutrophils)
Why suspicion is high (persistent fever despite antibiotics; characteristic skin lesions and/or positive cultures)
Need for additional tests to define extent and confirm organism/susceptibility
Posaconazole is an antifungal used to treat invasive mold infections; treatment may be prolonged and adjusted based on response and lab/culture results
Importance of adherence and correct administration (delayed-release tablets preferred; take with food if using suspension; do not interchange formulations without guidance)
Monitoring plan: drug levels (therapeutic drug monitoring), liver tests, kidney function, CBC, and repeat cultures if bloodstream infection
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening fever, new skin lesions, shortness of breath, vision changes, severe abdominal pain, jaundice, or signs of allergic reaction
Review allergies (penicillin rash; iodinated contrast nausea/vomiting) and clarify that posaconazole is unrelated to penicillins/contrast
Discuss potential side effects (GI upset, liver enzyme elevation, QT prolongation risk) and what monitoring will be done
Explain that drug interactions are common with posaconazole (strong CYP3A4 inhibitor) and all meds/supplements must be reviewed
Avoid starting new over-the-counter medications or herbal supplements without checking (interaction risk)
If neutropenic, reinforce infection-prevention measures per local policy (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts; food safety as instructed by oncology/hematology)
Keep follow-up appointments for labs and posaconazole level checks

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected fusariosis: immune status (neutropenia/hematologic malignancy/transplant/steroids), duration of fever, prior antibacterial/antifungal exposure, skin/eye/sinus/pulmonary symptoms, catheter presence, medication list (including QT-prolonging agents), pregnancy status, and baseline hepatic/renal disease.

Order diagnostic and baseline safety labs to support diagnosis and guide therapy: CBC with differential (neutropenia), CMP (AST/ALT/bilirubin/creatinine), blood cultures (including fungal if available), culture/biopsy of skin lesions with histopathology, susceptibility testing, and targeted imaging/tests based on symptoms (e.g., chest CT, sinus imaging, ophthalmology exam if eye symptoms).
Review results to confirm high suspicion/diagnosis, assess extent of disease, and establish baseline organ function for antifungal selection/monitoring.

Document working diagnosis of invasive fusariosis (or probable fusariosis) based on persistent fever despite antibiotics, compatible lesions, and/or positive cultures/histology; state need for susceptibility testing and evaluation for dissemination.
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify allergies and reaction types; confirm no azole allergy and address that penicillin/contrast reactions do not imply posaconazole allergy.

Critical safety check before prescribing: posaconazole is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and can interact with many drugs (e.g., calcineurin inhibitors, statins, certain benzodiazepines, antiarrhythmics); assess QT-prolonging combinations and adjust plan.

Critical safety check before prescribing: evaluate contraindications/precautions such as significant hepatic dysfunction, history of long-QT/arrhythmia risk, and concomitant contraindicated CYP3A4 substrates; ensure formulation-appropriate use.

Initiate posaconazole when appropriate (with correct formulation guidance), plan for prolonged therapy, and specify therapeutic drug monitoring and lab follow-up; include contingencies for lack of response or intolerance and need for specialist input.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis seriousness/dissemination risk in immunocompromised hosts; rationale for suspicion; need for further tests; explain posaconazole use, adherence and administration differences among formulations, monitoring plan, drug interaction risks, side effects, and urgent red flags.

Arrange close follow-up for repeat clinical assessment, lab monitoring (CBC/CMP), posaconazole trough level timing, repeat cultures if bloodstream infection, and coordination with infectious diseases/hematology.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

diagnosis.primary: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to CREBBP mutations

correct_diagnosis.primary: Iron deficiency anemia (microcytic anemia) in a patient with Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (CREBBP-related)
acceptable_alternatives: Microcytic anemia due to iron deficiency
Iron deficiency without anemia (early iron deficiency)
Anemia of chronic inflammation (if ferritin not low and iron/TIBC pattern consistent)
required_evidence: Microcytosis (low MCV) with elevated RDW and/or low hemoglobin on CBC
Low ferritin consistent with depleted iron stores (or ferritin borderline with iron studies supporting deficiency)
Low serum iron with elevated TIBC and low transferrin saturation supporting iron deficiency pattern
Clinical context supporting inadequate intake, blood loss, or malabsorption (history-based) and exclusion of alternative causes of microcytosis when indicated

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low (anemia) or low-normal if early iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal or elevated (reactive thrombocytosis can occur in iron deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low (most specific for iron deficiency if no active inflammation)

medications: Ferrous sulfate (elemental iron 3 mg/kg/day) PO once daily or every other day
If adult-sized dosing appropriate: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day
non_pharmacological: Identify and address cause: screen for dietary insufficiency and occult blood loss (e.g., heavy menses, GI bleeding); consider stool occult blood testing or GI evaluation if indicated by history
Dietary counseling: increase iron-rich foods (meat, legumes, fortified cereals) and vitamin C with iron; avoid taking iron with calcium/antacids/tea within 2 hours
Use behavioral/communication supports appropriate for Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (developmental delay): caregiver-supervised dosing, simplified instructions, adherence aids
Manage side effects: counsel on constipation/dark stools; consider stool softener if needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC (and optionally reticulocyte response) in 2-4 weeks; continue iron for at least 3 months after hemoglobin normalizes and ferritin repleted; repeat ferritin/iron studies at ~8-12 weeks to document repletion

must_include: Explain that labs suggest iron deficiency causing small red blood cells (microcytosis) and possibly anemia
Clarify that Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome does not itself directly treat the anemia but may affect diet, feeding, and follow-through
Explain the need to look for the reason iron is low (dietary intake vs blood loss vs absorption issues)
How to take iron (timing, with vitamin C, away from calcium/antacids); expected time course of improvement
Common side effects (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and how to manage them
Importance of continuing therapy for months after hemoglobin improves to rebuild iron stores
Safety counseling: keep iron out of reach of children due to overdose risk
Review allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate (urticarial rash) and latex (contact dermatitis) and reassure current plan avoids these
Discuss when to seek care urgently (severe abdominal pain, vomiting, signs of significant bleeding, worsening fatigue/syncope)
Dietary strategies to improve iron intake and absorption
Adherence plan tailored to developmental needs (caregiver involvement, routine, pill/liquid administration options)
Plan for follow-up labs and monitoring response

Perform required safety check before prescribing; review current meds/supplements for iron interactions (calcium, antacids/PPIs, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones, etc.).

Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment instructions/side effects, safety counseling (overdose risk), lifestyle/diet strategies, adherence plan tailored to developmental needs, and urgent return precautions.
Arrange follow-up and monitoring: repeat labs to assess response (e.g., retic/Hgb in 2–4 weeks; ferritin/iron stores after 2–3 months), and reassess etiology if poor response.

Agent avoids unsafe or inappropriate recommendations (e.g., parenteral iron without indication, excessive dosing, ignoring potential blood loss evaluation, or attributing anemia solely to Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome).

Open with: “Out of nowhere I got the worst headache of my life, like a thunderclap.” Volunteer that it peaked in under a minute, was so severe you had to stop, light/noise worsen it, and you’ve had a couple more similar sudden severe headaches over the past week.

Use of sympathomimetic decongestant (pseudoephedrine) as a potential trigger for vasoconstriction
Positive cannabinoid screen (cannabis exposure associated with RCVS; may confound with intoxication/withdrawal-related headache)
Mild CSF RBC elevation (can occur with small cortical SAH in RCVS or traumatic tap; complicates differentiation from aneurysmal SAH early in course)
Elevated blood pressure during pain episode (may be reactive vs primary hypertension contributing to hemorrhagic risk)

acceptable_alternatives: Thunderclap headache due to reversible cerebral vasoconstriction
required_evidence: Recurrent thunderclap headache(s) reaching maximal intensity within 1 minute
No aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage on initial evaluation (or hemorrhage pattern not consistent with ruptured aneurysm)
Cerebral angiography/CTA/MRA showing multifocal segmental cerebral arterial vasoconstriction ('string-of-beads')
Demonstration of reversibility of vasoconstriction within 12 weeks on repeat vascular imaging
No better alternative diagnosis (e.g., primary angiitis of the CNS, aneurysm rupture, cervical artery dissection)

non_pharmacological: Urgent neuroimaging pathway: non-contrast head CT to assess for subarachnoid hemorrhage; if CT negative and suspicion remains, lumbar puncture for xanthochromia (as clinically indicated)

Blood pressure and neurologic monitoring; treat severe hypertension per stroke protocols while avoiding hypotension
Consult Neurology; admit/observe if severe symptoms, neurologic deficits, hemorrhage/ischemia on imaging, or diagnostic uncertainty

Condition is usually reversible over weeks, but can cause complications such as stroke or brain hemorrhage
Need for imaging now and repeat vascular imaging later to confirm reversibility and exclude dangerous mimics

Pain control plan that avoids vasoconstrictive headache medicines (triptans/ergots) and sympathomimetics

nl_assertions: Agent elicits thunderclap characteristics (sudden onset with rapid peak), recurrence over days/weeks, and common triggers (exertion/sex/Valsalva).

Agent screens for high-risk alternative diagnoses and complications: SAH, dissection, CVST, meningitis/encephalitis, hypertensive emergency, and asks about focal deficits, seizure, neck pain/trauma, fever, anticoagulant use.

Agent identifies and documents likely RCVS triggers (postpartum state, vasoactive meds including triptans/ergots/decongestants, stimulants/cocaine, cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs) and recommends stopping/avoiding them with an individualized plan.
Agent states that RCVS is usually reversible over weeks but can cause ischemic stroke or intracranial hemorrhage and therefore needs urgent evaluation and monitoring.
Agent explicitly recommends immediate neuroimaging and vascular imaging now, plus repeat vascular imaging later to confirm reversibility and exclude dangerous mimics.
Before any prescribing, agent performs the required critical safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure of any is a critical error.

Agent avoids prescribing vasoconstrictive headache medications (triptans, ergots) and avoids sympathomimetics; if pain control is discussed, it uses non-vasoconstrictive options and warns about medication-overuse headache.

If a calcium-channel blocker is prescribed (e.g., nimodipine), agent counsels on hypotension/dizziness, advises blood pressure monitoring/hold parameters, and considers patient-specific risks (baseline low BP, interactions).
Agent provides clear return precautions: new weakness/numbness, trouble speaking, vision loss, seizure, confusion, or recurrence of worst headache; advises urgent/emergency evaluation.
Agent conducts shared decision-making about admission vs close observation based on symptoms, imaging findings, neurologic exam, and reliability of follow-up.
Agent gives lifestyle guidance: avoid exertional/sexual triggers and dehydration until cleared by Neurology; avoid analgesic overuse; adhere to follow-up imaging and neurology appointments.
Agent communication is understandable, non-alarmist but appropriately urgent, checks understanding, and documents a follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Stable patient with recurrent thunderclap headaches, no focal deficits, initial evaluation supports suspected RCVS; initiate CCB and non-vasoconstrictive analgesia with close follow-up and repeat imaging plan.
must_collect: sudden severe headache with rapid peak (thunderclap)
recurrent episodes over days to weeks
current medication list and OTC decongestants
baseline blood pressure and hypotension symptoms
steps: ASK: characterize thunderclap and recurrence; screen red flags and triggers; obtain meds/allergies
ORDER_LAB: basic labs +/- pregnancy test as appropriate; document vitals/BP
DIAGNOSE: suspected RCVS; explain need for imaging now and repeat vascular imaging later; discuss complications
PRESCRIBE: nimodipine (or alternative CCB per local practice) + non-vasoconstrictive pain plan; avoid triptans/ergots/sympathomimetics; counsel hypotension monitoring
EDUCATE: triggers to stop/avoid; expectations; urgent return precautions; lifestyle guidance; medication-overuse warning; shared decision-making on observation vs admission
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: neurology and repeat vascular imaging within weeks

minimal_paths.description: Suspected RCVS with concerning features (focal deficit, seizure, confusion, very high BP, or inability to ensure follow-up) prompting recommendation for urgent ED evaluation/admission while still meeting education and safety requirements.
must_collect: severe persistent headache or worsening pattern
baseline BP and hypertensive emergency symptoms
steps: ASK: identify red flags/complications and triggers; meds/allergies
